did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), EfficientNetV2 B0, and Vision Transformer (ViT B16), to classify skin lesion images from the ISIC dataset for skin cancer detection. The study evaluated these AI methods to achieve classification accuracy, with EfficientNetV2 achieving 88% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a study conducted at the 2023 6th International Conference on Information and Communications Technology (ICOIACT). It describes the methodology of using CNN, EfficientNetV2 B0, and ViT B16 models on the ISIC dataset, reports specific performance metrics (e.g., 88% accuracy for EfficientNet), and includes sections on Introduction, Related Work, Methods, Results, and Conclusion, which are characteristic of an original research article rather than a review.

title: Skin Cancer Classification Using EfficientNetV2 and ViT B16
journal: 2023 6th International Conference on Information and Communications Technology (ICOIACT)
brief_summary: This study addresses skin cancer classification by utilizing Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), EfficientNetV2 B0, and Vision Transformer (ViT B16) models on the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset. The researchers implemented preprocessing techniques to handle noisy data and applied data augmentation strategies to balance the distribution between benign and malignant lesion images. The models were evaluated using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score across different testing data proportions and epoch counts. The results demonstrated that EfficientNetV2 B0 achieved the highest performance with an accuracy of 88% and maintained stable generalization with 87% accuracy, effectively mitigating overfitting. Consequently, the study concludes that EfficientNetV2 and ViT B16 are promising approaches for improving the accuracy and reliability of automated skin cancer detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study utilized the ISIC dataset which contains images classified as benign (non-cancerous) and malignant. The experiment balanced the distribution between benign and malignant categories. Specific benign types mentioned in the introduction as part of the broader skin condition context include nevus, pigmented benign keratosis, seborrheic keratosis, dermatofibroma, and vascular lesions, but the experimental classification task was binary (benign vs. malignant).

The study utilized the ISIC dataset which contains images classified as benign and malignant (cancerous). The experiment balanced the distribution between benign and malignant categories. Specific malignant types mentioned in the introduction as part of the broader skin condition context include melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and actinic keratosis, but the experimental classification task was binary (benign vs. malignant).

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesion images into benign (non-cancerous) and malignant (cancerous) categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'Skin Cancer Classification' using the ISIC dataset. The dataset images are annotated as 'malignant (cancerous) or benign (non-cancerous)'. The loss function used is 'Binary cross-entropy', and performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score for class prediction. The study compares CNN, EfficientNetV2 B0, and ViT B16 models for this binary classification task.

datasets_used.name: International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC)
datasets_used.description: A well-regarded repository of images related to skin conditions, utilized in this research for skin cancer detection. The dataset contains skin lesion images annotated as malignant (cancerous) or benign (non-cancerous).

use.training: Used for training the CNN, EfficientNetV2 B0, and ViT B16 models after preprocessing (noise reduction, upscaling, color adjustment) and data augmentation (random horizontal/vertical rotation, random zoom) to balance the distribution between benign and malignant categories.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process (150 epochs) to monitor performance and prevent overfitting.
use.testing: Used for testing the models with varying testing data proportions (e.g., 0.2) and epoch counts to evaluate accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and compared qualitatively.

experiment_100_epochs_20_percent_test.notes: At 100 epochs with 20% testing data, EfficientNet_v2 achieved the highest accuracy among the three models.

experiment_150_epochs_15_percent_test.notes: EfficientNet_v2 and ViT_b16 maintained high performance. EfficientNet_v2 achieved 88.03% accuracy, while ViT_b16 achieved 87.88% accuracy. The paper notes these models' precision, recall, and F1 scores remained stable and higher than the CNN model's.

experiment_150_epochs_20_percent_test.notes: At 150 epochs with 20% testing data, ViT_b16 outperformed EfficientNet_v2 in accuracy (87.12% vs 86.67%). The CNN model experienced a decline in performance. The paper states EfficientNet_v2 maintained an accuracy of 87% in the abstract, likely referring to this or a similar stable generalization result.

architecture_used.main_model: Three models were used: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), EfficientNetV2 B0, and Vision Transformer (ViT B16).
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported for the custom CNN. EfficientNetV2 and ViT B16 are standard architectures often pretrained on ImageNet, but the paper does not explicitly state the pretraining source or weights used in this specific implementation.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported in the text.

layers: Convolutional layers with 16 filters and kernel_size=3
Additional convolutional layers with 8 filters and kernel_size=3
Dense layer with 128 units and ReLU activation

transformer_blocks: Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLP) with Gaussian Error Linear Unit (GELU) activation

ViT_B16.type: Not explicitly detailed beyond transformer encoder

preprocessing_techniques: Noise reduction to address noisy data in the dataset
Image resizing using the Lanczos3 method
Pixel value normalization by dividing by 255 to scale values between 0 and 1
Image upscaling to augment resolution and accentuate details
Color adjustments/modifications to optimize visualization and emphasize salient features

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied as part of the augmentation layer built using TensorFlow to increase training data variation.
Applied as part of the augmentation layer built using TensorFlow to increase training data variation.
Applied as part of the augmentation layer built using TensorFlow to increase training data variation and help improve the model's performance in recognizing similar image variations.

conclusions: EfficientNetV2 B0 and ViT B16 models consistently outperformed the custom CNN model in terms of accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score across different testing data proportions and epoch counts.

EfficientNetV2 B0 achieved the highest overall performance, reaching an accuracy of 88% (specifically 88.03% at 150 epochs with 15% test data) and demonstrating stable generalization with an accuracy of 87% (86.67% at 150 epochs with 20% test data), effectively mitigating overfitting.

ViT B16 also maintained high performance, achieving an accuracy of 87.88% (at 150 epochs with 15% test data) and 87.12% (at 150 epochs with 20% test data), outperforming EfficientNetV2 in the specific scenario with 20% testing data.

The CNN model experienced a decline in performance with increased epochs and testing data proportions, dropping to an accuracy of 80.30% at 150 epochs with 20% test data, whereas the deep learning models (EfficientNetV2 and ViT B16) maintained superior and stable results.

The study highlights the potential of EfficientNetV2 and ViT B16 as promising approaches for improving the accuracy and reliability of automated skin cancer detection, supporting early diagnosis and appropriate treatment.

Addressing dataset limitations such as noisy data, imbalanced classes, and segmentation accuracy through preprocessing (noise reduction, upscaling, color adjustment) and data augmentation is critical for enhancing the effectiveness of skin disease detection algorithms.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (benign vs. malignant) using the ISIC dataset, rather than multi-class differentiation of specific skin cancer subtypes (e.g., melanoma vs. basal cell carcinoma).
The dataset size is relatively small (3,297 images), which may limit the generalizability of the deep learning models compared to larger-scale clinical datasets.
No external validation was performed; the models were evaluated solely on the internal test splits of the ISIC dataset, raising concerns about external validity and potential dataset bias.
The paper does not report standard deviation or confidence intervals for the performance metrics, making it difficult to assess the statistical significance of the differences between models.
The custom CNN architecture is simple and shallow (few convolutional layers), which may not be a fair comparison to the complex, pre-trained EfficientNetV2 and ViT B16 architectures.

Data augmentation techniques (rotation, zoom) were applied, but the paper acknowledges that inaccurate augmentation can distort images; the specific impact of these augmentations on diagnostic fidelity is not quantitatively analyzed.
The study does not address clinical deployment challenges such as inference speed, computational resource requirements, or integration into clinical workflows.
No analysis of model interpretability or explainability (e.g., saliency maps) is provided, which is critical for clinical trust and validation in dermatology.
The paper mentions 'early stop' as a technique to prevent overfitting but does not specify the early stopping criteria or patience used in the experiments.
